chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly states in the Abstract and Introduction that a new Golang library was implemented for Marlin and HotStuff. However, after reviewing the entire text, including Section VI (Implementation and Evaluation), no working links to public repositories (e.g., GitHub/GitLab) or Docker containers are provided. The authors mention writing ~7,000 LOC but do not share the code.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation in Section VI describes a cluster of 40 commodity servers with specific hardware specs (16-core CPU, 128GB RAM). This constitutes a physical testbed deployment rather than just software simulation. The experiments measure throughput and latency on real hardware with injected network latency.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: Section III (System Model) contains a clear, explicit definition of the fault model. It specifies n ≥ 3f + 1 replicas, f Byzantine failures, and operates under a partially synchronous model with an unknown Global Stabilization Time (GST). The assumptions regarding cryptographic primitives are also formally defined.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: Section VI provides a rigorous, data-driven analysis of performance trade-offs. It quantifies throughput improvements over HotStuff in failure-free scenarios, scalability limits as 'f' increases, and view change latencies for both happy and unhappy paths under various failure conditions (1 and 3 failures).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.major_weakness: The paper fails to provide public access to the source code or datasets despite claiming an implementation was created, severely hindering reproducibility.
